title: Maya R. — First headache visit
description: 12-year-old with 4 months of recurrent headaches. Nonurgent migraine-compatible pattern, school impact, no red flags.

history.label: Well-child visit note
history.text: 12-year-old female, healthy, immunizations up to date. Mother mentions occasional headaches over the winter, not evaluated. Normal growth, normal neuro screening exam. BMI 50th percentile.

history.label: Nurse triage call
history.text: Parent called: Maya sent home from school twice this month with headache and nausea. Advised scheduling headache-focused visit.

history.label: Vitals (today)
history.text: BP 104/66, HR 78, T 36.8C, Ht 152 cm, Wt 43 kg. No fever. Appears well.

history.label: Medication list
history.text: No daily medications. PRN ibuprofen per parent report at check-in.

red_flag_catalog.label: Headache waking from sleep / worst on waking

pedmidas_questions.question: Full school days missed in last 3 months
Partial school days missed in last 3 months
School days functioning at less than half
Days unable to do home activities/chores
Days participated in activities at less than half

turns.speaker: clinician
turns.text: So Maya, tell me about these headaches. When did they start?

turns.speaker: patient
turns.text: Around the start of March, I guess. Like four months ago. They keep coming back.

evidence.quote: Around the start of March, I guess. Like four months ago. They keep coming back.

missing_questions: How many headache days per month?
How long does each headache last?
Where does it hurt and what does it feel like?
Any nausea, vomiting, light or sound sensitivity?
Any headaches that wake her from sleep?

turns.speaker: clinician
turns.text: How often are they happening now? How many days in a month would you say?

turns.speaker: parent
turns.text: It's gotten to maybe two a week. So eight days a month, sometimes more.

turns.speaker: patient
turns.text: They last a really long time too, like most of the afternoon until I sleep.

evidence.quote: It's gotten to maybe two a week. So eight days a month, sometimes more.
They last a really long time too, like most of the afternoon until I sleep.

missing_questions_remove: How many headache days per month?
How long does each headache last?

turns.speaker: clinician
turns.text: Where does it hurt, and what does it feel like?

turns.speaker: patient
turns.text: Mostly here on both sides of my forehead. It's like pounding, like a drum. Pretty bad — I can't really do homework when it happens.

evidence.quote: Mostly here on both sides of my forehead. It's like pounding, like a drum. Pretty bad — I can't really do homework when it happens.

missing_questions_remove: Where does it hurt and what does it feel like?

turns.speaker: clinician
turns.text: When the headache comes, does she feel sick to her stomach? Bothered by light or noise?

turns.speaker: parent
turns.text: Yes, she gets nauseous — she's thrown up once or twice. And she goes to her room and turns off the lights, says our voices are too loud.

evidence.quote: Yes, she gets nauseous — she's thrown up once or twice. And she goes to her room and turns off the lights, says our voices are too loud.

missing_questions_remove: Any nausea, vomiting, light or sound sensitivity?

turns.speaker: clinician
turns.text: Important questions: do headaches ever wake her up from sleep, or are they worst first thing in the morning? Any vomiting in the early morning? Weakness, numbness, trouble seeing or walking?

turns.speaker: parent
turns.text: No, never at night — they're always afternoon or evening. No morning vomiting. Nothing like weakness or vision problems.

turns.speaker: clinician
turns.text: And they came on gradually? No sudden worst-headache-of-her-life moment? No fevers or stiff neck?

turns.speaker: parent
turns.text: Gradual, yes. No, nothing sudden, no fevers.

evidence.quote: No, never at night — they're always afternoon or evening. No morning vomiting. Nothing like weakness or vision problems.
Gradual, yes. No, nothing sudden, no fevers.

missing_questions_remove: Any headaches that wake her from sleep?
missing_questions_add: Is the overall pattern stable or worsening month to month?

turns.speaker: clinician
turns.text: Compared to March, are they getting more frequent or stronger month over month, or about the same?

turns.speaker: parent
turns.text: They went up in the spring but the last two months it's been about the same. Not really worse.

evidence.quote: They went up in the spring but the last two months it's been about the same. Not really worse.

missing_questions_remove: Is the overall pattern stable or worsening month to month?

turns.speaker: clinician
turns.text: Let's talk about school. In the last three months, how many full days has she missed because of headaches? And days she went but left early?

turns.speaker: parent
turns.text: She missed four full days. And left early... I'd say six times.

turns.speaker: patient
turns.text: And a lot of days I'm there but I can't focus at all, like ten days maybe I got almost nothing done.

evidence.quote: She missed four full days. And left early... I'd say six times.
And a lot of days I'm there but I can't focus at all, like ten days maybe I got almost nothing done.

school_impact.value: Missed 4 full days, 6 partial days, ~10 low-function days in 3 months

missing_questions_add: Days unable to do chores or home activities (PedMIDAS)
Days missing or limited in sports/social activities (PedMIDAS)

turns.speaker: clinician
turns.text: Outside school — days she couldn't do things at home, chores, homework? And days she skipped soccer or hanging out with friends, or went but wasn't really herself?

turns.speaker: parent
turns.text: Maybe five days she just went straight to bed and did nothing at home. She skipped soccer practice three times, and probably four more where she played but badly and came off early.

evidence.quote: Maybe five days she just went straight to bed and did nothing at home. She skipped soccer practice three times, and probably four more where she played but badly and came off early.

missing_questions_remove: Days unable to do chores or home activities (PedMIDAS)
Days missing or limited in sports/social activities (PedMIDAS)
missing_questions_add: Sleep schedule, hydration, meals, screen time
What medications is she taking for attacks and how often?

turns.speaker: clinician
turns.text: Tell me about sleep, water, meals, screens.

turns.speaker: patient
turns.text: I go to sleep around midnight, sometimes later, on my phone. I skip breakfast a lot. I don't really drink water at school.

turns.speaker: clinician
turns.text: And what does she take when a headache hits?

turns.speaker: parent
turns.text: Ibuprofen. Honestly, probably three times a week lately. Sometimes it helps if she takes it early.

evidence.quote: I go to sleep around midnight, sometimes later, on my phone. I skip breakfast a lot. I don't really drink water at school.
Ibuprofen. Honestly, probably three times a week lately. Sometimes it helps if she takes it early.

missing_questions_remove: Sleep schedule, hydration, meals, screen time
What medications is she taking for attacks and how often?

turns.speaker: clinician
turns.text: Does anyone in the family get migraines?

turns.speaker: parent
turns.text: I did as a teenager, pretty bad ones. My mother too.

evidence.quote: I did as a teenager, pretty bad ones. My mother too.
BP 104/66, HR 78, T 36.8C. No fever. Appears well.

summary: 12-year-old with ~4 months of recurrent bilateral frontal pounding headaches, ~8 days/month, lasting hours. [ev-1, ev-2, ev-3, ev-4]
Attacks with nausea, occasional vomiting, photophobia and phonophobia; family history of migraine (mother, grandmother). [ev-5, ev-14]
Red-flag screen negative today: no waking headaches, no morning vomiting, no focal deficits, non-progressive pattern, normal vitals. [ev-6, ev-7, ev-8, ev-15]
PedMIDAS 32 (moderate disability): 4 full + 6 partial school days missed, reduced function at school, home, and sports. [ev-9, ev-10, ev-11]
Ibuprofen use ~3x/week — approaching medication-overuse range; lifestyle contributors present (late sleep, skipped breakfast, low hydration). [ev-13, ev-12]
questions_to_ask: Confirm exact ibuprofen dose and count per week
Any aura symptoms (visual changes before headache)?
suggested_pathway: Discuss migraine-compatible feature pattern with family (clinician review required)
Start structured headache diary with family (draft provided)
Lifestyle plan: regular sleep schedule, breakfast, hydration goal, screen limits at night
Review acute medication strategy; counsel on limiting NSAID use to <3 days/week
Consider preventive-pathway discussion if frequency/disability persists at follow-up
referral_considerations: No urgent neurology referral indicated today based on negative red-flag screen (clinician to confirm)
Refer if red flags emerge, pattern progresses, or disability remains moderate-high despite 8–12 weeks of initial plan
patient_instructions: Keep the headache diary daily — one tap per day
Same bedtime every night, phone out of the bedroom
Eat breakfast; carry a water bottle at school
Take ibuprofen early in an attack, but not more than 3 days per week
Return sooner if headaches wake her at night, come with vomiting in the morning, or change suddenly
care_plan.disclaimer: Draft for clinician review. Final clinical decisions remain with the clinician.

canned_qa.answer: The family reported Maya missed 4 full school days and left early 6 times in the last 3 months, plus about 10 days where she was present but got almost nothing done. This is captured in PedMIDAS items 1–3.

All screened red flags are marked absent based on today's interview: no waking/morning headaches, no early-morning vomiting, no focal neurologic symptoms, no thunderclap onset, no fever/stiff neck, and a non-progressive pattern over the last two months.

Per parent report, Maya takes ibuprofen about 3 times per week for attacks, which approaches the medication-overuse range and is flagged for clinician review. Exact dosing was not confirmed during the visit.
The mother reported having significant migraines as a teenager, and the maternal grandmother had them as well.
